content.text: A Definition of Done (DoD) for agent capability delivery is the shared agreement on what conditions must be met before a new agent feature or capability is considered complete and ready for production. Without a formal DoD, 'done' is defined differently by every engineer on every story, and agent features routinely ship without observability instrumentation, without adversarial eval coverage, or without documented rollback procedures — creating operational debt that the on-call team inherits the moment the feature goes live.
What it is

A Definition of Done for agent capability delivery is a team-agreed checklist of completion criteria that every agent feature must satisfy before it is merged to the main branch and deployed to production. It extends the general software engineering DoD to address concerns specific to agentic AI: adversarial evaluation coverage, safety reviewer sign-off, cost and token budget validation, trace instrumentation, and action scope documentation. The DoD is a living document owned by the engineering team, maintained with input from AI safety, SRE, and product.

The DoD checklist is organized into six categories: functional correctness (tests and eval pass), safety and alignment (adversarial evaluation and human safety review), observability (traces, metrics, alerts, and runbook), documentation (capability description, API docs, changelog), deployment readiness (feature flag, rollback procedure, staging validation), and cost and performance (token budget, latency SLA, cost estimate). A feature is 'done' only when every item across all six categories is checked — partial completion does not count.
Why it matters

Agent capabilities have a broader operational surface than typical software features. A new agent capability introduces not just code behavior but also: a cost surface (token usage that accumulates per request), a safety surface (new ways the agent can be prompted or misbehave), an observability gap (new execution paths that must be instrumented to be debugged), and a rollback challenge (if the capability interacts with external systems, rollback is more complex than reverting a code change). A DoD that covers all these dimensions prevents the team from shipping capabilities that look complete from a code perspective but are operationally incomplete.

The DoD also creates a shared definition of quality across the team. Without a formal DoD, different engineers have different intuitions about what 'done' means, and junior engineers may not know to consider safety evaluation or cost budgets when implementing a feature. The DoD makes the full quality bar explicit and learnable, raising the floor for the whole team rather than relying on each engineer's individual expertise.
Benefits
Prevents incomplete features from reaching production: the DoD acts as a final checkpoint that catches missing test coverage, unwritten runbooks, and unvalidated cost estimates before deployment.
Creates shared quality standards: every engineer on the team applies the same completion criteria, eliminating variation in what 'done' means across different feature contributors.
Reduces operational debt: by requiring observability, runbooks, and rollback procedures as part of done, the DoD ensures on-call engineers have the tools they need from day one.
Enforces safety coverage: the adversarial evaluation and safety reviewer requirements ensure no new agent capability ships without a minimum safety validation threshold.
Improves sprint planning: a clear DoD makes story-point estimation more accurate by ensuring teams account for the full work scope, including documentation and instrumentation.
Audit and compliance: the completed DoD checklist provides evidence that each feature was reviewed for safety, cost, and operational readiness before deployment.
Trade-offs and limitations
Sprint velocity perception: tracking all DoD items increases the measured effort per story, which may create pressure to reduce DoD requirements rather than increase sprint capacity.
Context specificity: a uniform DoD applied to all agent changes may be too burdensome for minor capability extensions and too lightweight for major new agent types; tiered DoDs add complexity.
False completion: engineers may check DoD items as complete when they are only partially satisfied (e.g., 'runbook written' when only a skeleton exists), requiring tech lead review to catch.
DoD maintenance: the DoD itself needs maintenance; outdated items create confusion and reduce trust in the checklist as the team's understanding of what matters evolves.
Cross-team dependencies: some DoD items (safety sign-off, SRE review of runbook) require input from other teams with their own schedules, making DoD completion dependent on external availability.
How it works

The DoD is implemented as a checklist in the team's project management tool and as a PR template section. When an engineer considers a story complete, they complete the DoD self-review: they go through each category and mark items as complete, not-applicable (with justification), or blocked (with the blocker identified). The tech lead then reviews the self-assessment, spot-checking any items marked complete that are not directly verifiable from the PR (such as 'runbook written and linked' or 'cost estimate reviewed with PM').

Items in the safety category (adversarial eval, safety reviewer sign-off) are verified against the PR's required status checks and CODEOWNERS approvals, which are already enforced by branch protection. Items in the observability category are verified by checking the PR diff for trace span creation at new code paths, alert definition files, and a link to the updated dashboard. Items in the documentation and deployment readiness categories require the engineer to provide links: to the updated documentation page, the feature flag configuration, and the rollback procedure.

The completed DoD checklist is attached to the PR (or linked as a comment) and becomes part of the PR's audit record. A tech lead signs off on the DoD as a final approval step before the PR can be merged. This approval is separate from the peer code review and the safety review — it specifically validates that the completion checklist is fully and genuinely satisfied, not just that the code logic is correct.
Architecture

attrs.alt: DoD checklist showing six categories: functional correctness, safety, observability, documentation, deployment readiness, cost/performance

content.text: The diagram shows the six DoD categories and the four-step completion flow. Every category must be fully satisfied before deployment is permitted. The bottom flow shows the sequence from developer self-review through tech lead sign-off to deploy. This structure prevents any single category from being de-prioritized; a feature that passes code review but lacks observability instrumentation cannot be deployed until the instrumentation is added.
When to use it

The full DoD applies to all agent capabilities that will be deployed to a production environment where they interact with real users or external systems. A lightweight DoD (covering only functional correctness and basic observability) may be appropriate for prototypes in isolated development environments. The full DoD is mandatory before any capability crosses from a staging environment to production, regardless of whether it is labeled a 'beta', 'experimental', or 'limited' release.

Teams should discuss and agree on the DoD at the beginning of each quarter or when significant changes are made to the agent architecture. The DoD should be visible in the team's engineering handbook and referenced in sprint planning so that stories are scoped with DoD work included in the estimate.
How to implement
Draft the initial DoD in a collaborative session with engineering, AI safety, SRE, and product; categorize items into the six domains and prioritize ruthlessly to keep the list to 20-30 items total.
Add the DoD as a required section in the PR template so that every agent feature PR includes an explicit DoD completion status.
Create a definition card in the team's project management tool that links to the full DoD document; link the card from all agent feature story templates.
Identify which DoD items can be verified automatically (eval pass via CI, trace spans present via code review) and which require tech lead review (runbook quality, rollback procedure adequacy).
Configure CODEOWNERS and branch protection to enforce the technical DoD items that can be gated in CI (eval, lint, safety review approval).
Add DoD completion status to the sprint review checklist: before marking a story as done in the sprint, the tech lead must confirm the DoD is complete.
Conduct a DoD retrospective every quarter: review which items were most commonly incomplete at first submission and identify whether process or tooling changes can address the root cause.
Publish a DoD exceptions log: when a story ships with a DoD item deferred, record the item, the justification, the owner, and the target resolution date.
Examples

## Definition of Done — Agent Capability Checklist

### Functional Correctness
- [ ] Unit tests cover the new capability path (happy path + at least 2 edge cases)
- [ ] Integration tests exercise the full agent call chain for the new capability
- [ ] Eval regression suite passes at or above the current baseline score (link: )
- [ ] Tool output schemas are validated; malformed outputs produce explicit errors, not silent failures

### Safety and Alignment
- [ ] Adversarial eval suite run against this capability: 0 new failure scenarios (link: )
- [ ] Safety reviewer has approved the prompt and tool changes (PR approval by @ai-safety-reviewers)
- [ ] Action scope documented and reviewed: the capability cannot perform actions beyond the stated scope
- [ ] Output filtering and content policy checks are in place if the capability produces user-visible text

### Observability
- [ ] Every model call in this capability path logs: prompt hash, model version, input tokens, output tokens, latency, cost
- [ ] Distributed trace spans created for all new code paths; trace IDs propagated from HTTP request to tool calls
- [ ] Dashboard updated or created to show this capability's request rate, error rate, latency, and cost (link: )
- [ ] Alert rule created for capability failure rate > 1% over a 5-minute window (link: )
- [ ] Runbook written: describes what the capability does, how to diagnose failures, and how to disable it (link: )

### Documentation
- [ ] Capability description added to the agent capabilities index in the developer portal
- [ ] API or configuration reference updated if the capability changes the agent's interface
- [ ] Changelog entry written describing the new capability and any behavioral changes for existing users

### Deployment Readiness
- [ ] Feature flag configured in the flag management service; capability defaults to OFF
- [ ] Rollback procedure documented and tested in staging: describes how to disable or revert the capability
- [ ] Capability has been validated in the staging environment with realistic traffic patterns

### Cost and Performance
- [ ] Token budget estimated and within the per-request target for this agent tier
- [ ] Latency p95 measured in staging; confirmed within the latency SLA for this agent (link: )
- [ ] Cost-per-request estimate reviewed and approved by PM or cost owner (link: )

# Example: DoD items mapped to CI enforcement
# .github/workflows/dod-checks.yml
name: DoD Validation
on:
  pull_request:
    paths: ['src/agents/**', 'src/tools/**', 'src/prompts/**']

jobs:
  functional-correctness:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pytest tests/unit tests/integration --tb=short

  eval-regression:
    name: eval-suite  # required status check
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: python scripts/run_eval_suite.py --threshold 0.97

  observability-check:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Check trace spans in new code
        run: |
          # Ensure new agent code uses the tracing decorator
          python scripts/check_trace_coverage.py src/agents/ src/tools/
      - name: Verify cost logging present
        run: |
          python scripts/check_cost_logging.py src/agents/ src/tools/
Real-world example

A healthcare technology company building an agent for clinical documentation struggled with a pattern where features shipped to staging and were then held up for weeks adding observability and runbooks that had not been included in the original implementation. Each feature was 'code complete' but operationally incomplete, and the SRE team would not approve deployment without the missing instrumentation.

After adopting a formal DoD that included observability and deployment readiness as mandatory completion criteria, the team restructured feature stories to include explicit subtasks for trace instrumentation, alert creation, and runbook writing. These subtasks were estimated separately and included in the sprint capacity calculation. The first quarter after adoption saw a 40% reduction in staging dwell time (time between staging deployment and production approval), because features arrived in staging already fully instrumented and documented. The SRE review step became a validation check rather than a gap-filling exercise.
Common mistakes
Treating the DoD as a final checkbox after the feature is 'code complete' rather than as a set of constraints that shape implementation decisions from the start.
Including so many items in the DoD that it becomes unwieldy; engineers start ignoring sections or marking items without genuine review.
Marking observability items complete when only the code is present but the dashboard and alerts have not been verified to work end-to-end.
Deferring DoD items to 'the next sprint' without recording them as known exceptions, causing them to slip indefinitely.
Not including the DoD item effort in sprint story estimates, causing the team to consistently run over capacity when they account for the full completion work.
Allowing tech lead sign-off to be waived under delivery pressure; this removes the independent verification step that catches items marked complete incorrectly.
Best practices

Integrate DoD items into the story acceptance criteria at creation time, not as a separate review step after development; this ensures implementation choices account for DoD requirements from the start.
Create reusable DoD templates for common capability types (new tool integration, new agent type, prompt-only change) with pre-populated items relevant to each type.
Track DoD item completion rates across sprints and share with the team in retrospectives: which categories are most often incomplete at first submission and why.
Automate all technically verifiable DoD items; the human review step should focus on the judgment-requiring items that automation cannot validate.
Define a clear escalation path for DoD items blocked on external teams: if safety sign-off is delayed, the item is not waived — the story waits or the escalation path is used.
Review and update the DoD annually or after any production incident where a missing DoD item was a contributing factor.
Performance and scalability

A well-structured DoD adds approximately 20-30% to feature development time when first adopted, as teams invest in instrumentation and documentation that was previously skipped. This overhead decreases as teams internalize DoD requirements and build reusable infrastructure (shared base classes with built-in tracing, template runbooks, feature flag libraries) that make DoD completion faster. After six to twelve months, the overhead stabilizes at approximately 10-15%, and the reduction in post-deployment incidents and staging dwell time typically more than offsets this investment.

The observability DoD items scale particularly well: each new capability that arrives with pre-built dashboards and alerts reduces the operational burden on the SRE team proportionally. A team that ships twenty capabilities per quarter, each with fully built observability, creates a monitoring system that grows with the product rather than falling behind it.

Security and reliability

The DoD's safety category items (adversarial evaluation, safety reviewer sign-off, action scope review) collectively function as a minimum viable safety gate for new agent capabilities. By requiring these to be complete before deployment, the DoD prevents capabilities from entering production without a baseline safety validation — even if the team is under pressure to ship quickly.

The deployment readiness items (rollback procedure, feature flag, staging validation) directly improve reliability. An agent capability deployed without a tested rollback procedure leaves the team unable to respond quickly to production issues. The DoD ensures every capability has an off-switch (feature flag) and a tested path back to the previous state, enabling rapid mitigation of post-deployment incidents.
DoD exceptions and waivers

Not every DoD item is applicable to every feature, and rigid application of the full checklist to minor changes creates friction without proportionate safety benefit. A DoD exception process allows an item to be waived for a specific feature with documented justification, owner approval, and a resolution plan. Common legitimate exceptions include: a capability deployed behind a feature flag to a 0.1% canary audience where full cost impact cannot be measured in staging, or a time-critical security patch where the runbook will be written within 24 hours of deployment.

Exceptions are not informal agreements. Each exception is recorded in the story's DoD checklist with: the item being excepted, the reason it cannot be completed before deployment, the name of the tech lead or engineering manager who approved the exception, and the date by which the exception will be resolved. Unresolved exceptions older than the agreed resolution date are surfaced in the weekly engineering quality review and escalated to the engineering manager. This process prevents exceptions from becoming permanent deferrals.
DoD and sprint velocity

Introducing a comprehensive DoD typically causes a temporary decrease in measured sprint velocity as teams account for DoD work that was previously untracked. This decrease is real and should be explained honestly to stakeholders: the team is now completing features to a higher standard that prevents operational debt accumulation. The appropriate response is to recalibrate velocity expectations for agent features, not to reduce DoD requirements.

Over two to three quarters, velocity stabilizes as teams develop reusable infrastructure that makes DoD completion faster: shared base classes with built-in trace instrumentation (eliminating the observability implementation cost for each feature), template runbooks (reducing runbook writing time), and standard feature flag patterns (simplifying the deployment readiness work). The initial velocity reduction is an investment in a higher steady-state delivery rate once DoD friction is engineered away.
Connecting DoD to incident learning

When a production incident occurs that is attributable to an agent capability, the post-mortem should include a DoD audit: which DoD items were complete at the time of deployment, and which items, if complete, would have prevented or mitigated the incident. This audit performs two functions: it provides specific, actionable findings for the incident remediation (complete the missing DoD items before re-enabling the capability), and it informs DoD evolution (if a gap not covered by any DoD item contributed to the incident, a new item should be added).

Over time, the DoD accumulates evidence of its own effectiveness: a library of incidents prevented by DoD items (where a gate caught an issue before deployment) and incidents caused by DoD items that were incomplete or absent (where the gap in the DoD was a contributing factor). This evidence base makes the case for DoD investment concrete and resists pressure to simplify the DoD under delivery pressure. Teams that can point to three incidents that a single DoD item prevented will maintain that item through periods of velocity pressure; teams that cannot articulate the DoD's value are more vulnerable to erosion.
Making the DoD visible in the development workflow

A DoD that lives in a wiki page that engineers only consult when asked is a DoD that does not function. For the DoD to shape engineering behavior, it must be embedded in the tools engineers use during development: the story creation template, the PR template, the sprint review checklist, and the deployment runbook. Each of these touchpoints reinforces the DoD at a different point in the feature lifecycle.

Some teams create a DoD companion checklist that they work through during development rather than only at completion. Each morning stand-up for a story in progress, the engineer answers: which DoD items have I completed since yesterday, and which DoD items will I work on today? This daily visibility keeps DoD work from being deferred to the final day of a sprint. When engineers consistently answer 'no DoD items today' for multiple standups, it is a signal that the story was scoped without DoD work in the estimate — a planning gap to correct in the next sprint's estimation session.

Quarterly, the team reviews the DoD in a dedicated session and answers three questions: are there items that are never completed before the first staging deployment (suggesting they need to move earlier in the workflow), are there items that are always marked complete but never actually verified (suggesting they need better tooling or clearer criteria), and are there new failure modes from the past quarter that the DoD does not yet cover? This review cycle keeps the DoD accurate, actionable, and effective as the team and the system evolve.
Key takeaways
A DoD for agent capabilities must cover six areas: functional correctness, safety and alignment, observability, documentation, deployment readiness, and cost/performance.
The DoD is not a final checklist at the end of development — it is a set of constraints that shape implementation choices throughout the feature's development.
All six categories must be complete before a feature is deployed; partial completion creates operational debt that the on-call team inherits.
Automate all technically verifiable DoD items; reserve human review for judgment-requiring items like runbook quality and cost estimate adequacy.
Include DoD completion work in story estimates to prevent teams from consistently running over capacity when accounting for documentation and instrumentation.
Review the DoD quarterly and after production incidents to ensure it reflects the team's current understanding of what 'done' means for agent capabilities.
